topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red 'D' stared back at me from the graded essay. I picked up the paper, the cheap printer paper crinkling under my fingertips. It was barely a passing mark, a statistical anomaly in the sea of C’s and B’s. I didn't even bother to reread the feedback; the professor's scribbles looked like meaningless squiggles. I slid the paper into my backpack without a second glance, the weight of it negligible. I walked to my desk, pulled out my phone, and started scrolling through social media. The world outside the confines of this disappointing grade seemed utterly flat, the colors muted. There was no surge of disappointment, just a slow, steady acknowledgment of another expected outcome. I just needed to get to the next thing.

[story 2]

My stomach clenched, a tiny, almost imperceptible tightening that vanished as quickly as it appeared. When I saw the numerical score—a dismal 42 percent—I simply blinked. It registered, a fact to be processed and filed away. I didn't frown, didn't slump. I just leaned back in my chair, letting my head rest against the cool plastic of the monitor. The room felt strangely quiet, the ambient noise of the bustling lecture hall fading into a dull hum. I picked up my pen, not to correct anything, but just to move it across the page, the motion automatic, mechanical. What happened next was irrelevant; the focus was already drifting toward the next assignment due next week.

[story 3]

"Well, that's a surprise," I mumbled, the words barely audible as I handed the paper to the TA. My shoulders remained relaxed, almost slumped, a posture of utter non-engagement. I watched the TA collect the work, noting the slight, almost imperceptible lift at the corners of their mouth—a flicker of mild surprise, perhaps. I felt nothing significant about the outcome. It was just data. I gathered my books, the sound of the shuffling textbooks a monotonous backdrop to my internal monologue, which was already formulating plans for the weekend. The grade was just another data point in a long, unremarkable sequence of academic experiences.
